perturbation.original_text: 3. Return of Information Upon the request of the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confide ntial Information at Disclosing Party’s request, or at Disclosing Party’s option, certify destruction of the same.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Return of Information Upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confide ntial Information within 30 days of Disclosing Party’s request, unless mutually agreed in writing to retain certain information for ongoing Transaction purposes. If the Receiving Party is requested to return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information, the Disclosing Party does not have the option to certify the destruction of the same.
perturbation.explanation: The Return of Information section has been modified to add a 30-day period for returning the information after receiving the request. Also, the Receiving party can retain certain information. This is contradictory with Section 5: 'All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of Disclosing Party', if confidential information is retained, the property of the Information is not to the Disclosing party if the receiving party is retaining it and/or using it for the business.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. No Further Rights All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of Disclosing Party. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed to as granting or conferring any rights in the Confidential Information except as provide d herein.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception allowing the Receiving Party to retain confidential information for ongoing transaction purposes, which contradicts the original statement in Section 5 that 'All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of Disclosing Party'. This creates a direct conflict because if the Receiving Party retains and uses the confidential information, the information cannot fully remain the property of the Disclosing Party.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Injunctive Relief Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confident ial Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive and other equitable relief (without bond and without the necessity of showing actual monetary damages) as may be deemed proper by a court.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Injunctive Relief Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information if said disclosure causes material harm to the Disclosing Party, and that Disclosing Party may be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to seek injunctive and other equitable relief (with bond and with the necessity of showing actual monetary damages) as may be deemed proper by a court. The Disclosing Party will not be entitled to seek injunctive relief if they did not take the necessary precautions to classify the information appropriately.
perturbation.explanation: The Injunctive Relief section has been modified to say, 'if said disclosure causes material harm to the Disclosing Party, and that Disclosing Party may be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to seek injunctive and other equitable relief (with bond and with the necessity of showing actual monetary damages) as may be deemed proper by a court. The Disclosing Party will not be entitled to seek injunctive relief if they did not take the necessary precautions to classify the information appropriately.'This directly contradicts the previous statement within the same section that injunctive relief is available 'without bond and without the necessity of showing actual monetary damages' This creates an in-text contradiction regarding the conditions for obtaining injunctive relief.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Injunctive Relief Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information if said disclosure causes material harm to the Disclosing Party, and that Disclosing Party may be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to seek injunctive and other equitable relief (with bond and with the necessity of showing actual monetary damages) as may be deemed proper by a court. The Disclosing Party will not be entitled to seek injunctive relief if they did not take the necessary precautions to classify the information appropriately.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that injunctive relief would be available 'without bond and without the necessity of showing actual monetary damages.' The changed text contradicts this by stating that injunctive relief may be sought 'with bond and with the necessity of showing actual monetary damages' under certain conditions, and also adds another condition where injunctive relief will not be granted if the disclosing party did not classify the information appropriately, creating a direct conflict within the same section.

perturbation.original_text: 5. No Further Rights All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of Disclosing Party. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed to as granting or conferring any rights in the Confidential Information except as provide d herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. No Further Rights All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of Disclosing Party, unless otherwise agreed to in writing. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed to as granting or conferring any rights in the Confidential Information except as provide d herein.
perturbation.explanation: Section 5 has been modified to say: 'All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of Disclosing Party, unless otherwise agreed to in writing.' This contradicts Section 3, which dictates the Receiving party must return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confide ntial Information at Disclosing Party’s request, or at Disclosing Party’s option, certify destruction of the same. By adding the exception, it becomes unclear when the Receiving Party must return or destroy the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Return of Information Upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confide ntial Information within 30 days of Disclosing Party’s request, unless mutually agreed in writing to retain certain information for ongoing Transaction purposes. If the Receiving Party is requested to return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information, the Disclosing Party does not have the option to certify the destruction of the same.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 5 introduces an exception ('unless otherwise agreed to in writing') to the requirement that Confidential Information remains the property of the Disclosing Party. This contradicts Section 3, which specifies the conditions under which the Receiving Party must return or destroy confidential information. The added exception in Section 5 creates ambiguity about when the Receiving Party must return or destroy the information, thus creating an in-text contradiction, satisfying the condition for Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. No Commitment . The parties expressly agree that the provision of Information hereunder and discussions held in connection with the Transaction shall not prevent either party from pursuing similar discussions with third parties or obligate either party to continue discussions with the other or to take, continue or forego any action relating to the Transaction . Any estimates or forecasts provided by either party to the other shall not constitute commitments.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. No Commitment . The parties expressly agree that the provision of Information hereunder and discussions held in connection with the Transaction shall not prevent either party from pursuing similar discussions with third parties, except that neither party shall disclose the specific terms of this Agreement to any third party during such discussions, or obligate either party to continue discussions with the other or to take, continue or forego any action relating to the Transaction . Any estimates or forecasts provided by either party to the other shall not constitute commitments.
perturbation.explanation: Section 6 has been modified to add a clause: 'except that neither party shall disclose the specific terms of this Agreement to any third party during such discussions'. This directly contradicts Section 1, where it is stated that Confidential Information shall not include any information that (i) is or subsequently becomes publicly available without Receiving Party’s breach of any obligation owed to Disclosing Party, since the 'specific terms of this Agreement' are also being considered as part of the 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Confidential Information “Confidential Information” means nonpublic information that disclosing party (“Disclosing Party”) designates as being confidential. Confidential Information includes, without limitation, non -public information relating to released or unreleased Disclosing Party software pro ducts, the marketing or promotion of any Disclosing Party product, Disclosing Party’s business policies or practices, financial information, technical information, computer systems, infrastructure designs, data, analysis, compilations, studies or other doc umentation and information received from others that Disclosing Party is obligated to treat as confidential. Confidential Information disclosed to Receiving Party by any Disclosing Party, its related entities and/or agents is covered by this Agreement. C onfidential Information shall not include any information that: (i) is or subsequently becomes publicly available without Receiving Party’s breach of any obligation owed to Disclosing Party; (ii) became known to Receiving Party prior to Disclosing Party’s disclosure of such information to Receiving Party; (iii) became known to Receiving Party from a source other than Disclosing Party other than by the breach of an obligation of confidentiali ty owed to Disclosing Party; (iv) is independently developed by Rec eiving Party without access to the Disclosing Party’s information , or (v ) the Confidential Information is required to be disclosed pursuant to a requirement of a governmental agency or law so long as the other party is provided notice of such requirement p rior to any such disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 6 now prevents the disclosure of 'specific terms of this Agreement' to third parties during discussions. This directly contradicts Section 1, which states that Confidential Information shall not include information that 'is or subsequently becomes publicly available without Receiving Party’s breach of any obligation owed to Disclosing Party'. Since the 'specific terms of this Agreement' are now explicitly prohibited from disclosure in Section 6, it creates an in-text contradiction, based on the explanation provided.
